retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Sphenoid Bone -- Review Questions
retrieved_docs.content: Access free multiple choice questions on this topic. Comment on this article.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Sphenoid Bone -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The sphenoid is just one of the twenty-two bones that form the skull and essentially helps to connect the neurocranium to the facial skeleton. It is a single bone in the midline of the cranial cavity situated posterior to the frontal bone but anterior to the occipital. Its name derives from the Greek ' sphenoeides, ' which means wedge-shaped. It has several foramina and fissures present in its surface that allow the entry and exit of nerves and blood vessels to and from the cranial cavity. Shaped like a butterfly or a bat, it has a central body and two lateral wings on either side. It is one of the bones that make up the orbit, in particular, forming the posterior surface. The articulations of the sphenoid are with the frontal, parietal, ethmoid, zygomatic, temporal, occipital, palatine, and vomer bones. There may be several morphological variations in the relationship between the sphenoid, the ethmoid, and the frontal bone. Together, these give rigidity and stability to the skull making it the ideal place to house the brain. [1] [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Sphenoid Bone -- Structure and Function -- Structure
retrieved_docs.content: The sphenoid broadly divides into the central body, the greater wings, the lesser wings, and the pterygoid process. The body of the sphenoid is the medial part of the bone, in between the two lateral greater wings. It contains a depression called the sella turcica, which has a central depression for the pituitary gland. The body also houses the sphenoidal sinuses. The two lesser wings of the sphenoid are present anterior to the body and are triangular. The two greater wings of the sphenoid are also triangular in shape and are lateral to the body. The pterygoid process is directed downwards and made up of two laminae. [3] [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Sphenoid Bone -- Clinical Significance -- Sphenoid Fractures:
retrieved_docs.content: Fractures of the sphenoid occur in cases of trauma to the orbit or the base of the skull. These fractures may present with multiple neurological sequelae as the sphenoid is closely associated with numerous nerves that pass through it. Loss of vision and ocular damage is closely related to these fractures. Signs and symptoms include Battle sign, hemotympanum, and cranial nerve palsies, among others.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Sphenoid Bone -- Surgical Considerations
retrieved_docs.content: Trans-sphenoidal surgery is the therapeutic choice in cases of pituitary tumors that require surgical removal. It involves accessing the pituitary gland by passing surgical instruments through the nose and sphenoidal sinuses to reach the sella turcica. This trans-sphenoidal approach may also be an access method to perform surgeries on other structures apart from the pituitary, like in the case of a craniopharyngioma.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Orbit Bones -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The following seven bones form the orbit: Sphenoid Frontal Zygomatic Ethmoid Lacrimal Maxilla Palatine

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Nose Paranasal Sinuses -- Structure and Function -- Sphenoid Sinus
retrieved_docs.content: The sphenoid sinus is located centrally and posteriorly within the body of the sphenoid bone [16] , and the sella turcica posteriorly and superiorly bounds it. The sphenoid sinus, present only in humans and primates, [16] can be identified in radiographs from the age of 2. [17] It keeps developing throughout life but matures in size at around 12 to 14 years of age. [16] The typical adult size is 0.5 to 8 mL. Several important structures are closely related to the sphenoid sinus, including the internal carotid artery and the optic nerve. [18] The carotid artery is located adjacent to the lateral wall of the sinus, and in 25% of patients, it is dehiscent in this area. The optic nerve leaves an anteroposterior indentation on the roof of the sphenoid, and the overlying bone can be dehiscent in around 4% of individuals. [18] The sphenoid sinus is also adjacent to the cavernous sinus and hypophysial gland. [16] The patterns of the sphenoidal sinus pneumatization were classified into conchal, pre-sellar, and sellar, depending on the extension of pneumatization concerning the sella turcica. But, Štokovic et al added a fourth type – post-sellar. [19] The sphenoid ostium drains into the sphenoethmoidal recess located within the superior meatus.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Sphenoid Bone -- Structure and Function -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: Sphenoid bone has many essential functions. It helps form the base and lateral sides of the skull in combination with the orbital floor. Its many articulations with other bones give the skull rigidity. It is an attachment site for many of the muscles of mastication. A portion of the sphenoid forms the facial skeleton. Also, several fissures and foramina exist in the sphenoid, which transports several blood vessels and nerves of the skull to the head and neck.  The body of the sphenoid has a cavity with a sinus that communicates with the nasal cavity. These sphenoidal sinuses work to make the skull lightweight and to increase resonance.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Asterion -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: The asterion is an anatomical landmark on the lateral aspect of the skull formed at the junction of the occipital bone, the temporal bone, and the parietal bone (see Image. Asterion Landmark). Its definition can also be a confluence of the lambdoid suture, which is the junction of the occipital and parietal bones, the occipitomastoid suture, which is a junction of the occipital and temporal bones, and the parietomastoid suture, which is a junction of the parietal and temporal bones. It is a visible landmark on the human skull that is seen when the removal of the soft tissue from the bony surfaces takes place. Asterion is internally related to the transverse and sigmoid sinuses. It signifies the site of the closed mastoid fontanelle. Asterion corresponds to the posterior end of the parietotemporal suture, the Pterion forming the anterior end. It is used as a surface landmark for the radiological and anthropological measurement of the skull. The sutural anatomy of the asterion is useful in retrosigmoid surgical approaches to the posterior fossa. [1] The location of the asterion has been studied in various population demographics, trying to demonstrate whether it can be used as a reliable landmark for surgical approaches.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Sphenopalatine Foramen -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The sphenopalatine foramen (SPF) represents an opening on the lateral nasal wall that is present at the articulation between the superior aspect of the vertical portion of the palatine bone and the inferior projection of the sphenoid bone . [1] The sphenopalatine foramen connects the nasal cavity to the pterygopalatine fossa of the skull and houses the sphenopalatine artery (SPA) and vein, the posterior superior lateral nasal nerve, and the nasopalatine nerves. [2] [3] [2] Moreover, the sphenopalatine foramen provides an important anatomical landmark for those who perform procedures where knowledge of the sphenopalatine foramen and its contents remains critical, including the creation of a nasoseptal flap for nasal reconstruction or skull base repairs,  posterior epistaxis control, and functional endoscopic sinonasal surgery. Herein we discuss the anatomy of the sphenopalatine foramen, including its structure, function, contents, embryology, anatomical variations, and clinical relevance.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Sphenoid Bone -- Embryology
retrieved_docs.content: The sphenoid bone is a complicated structure in terms of its embryologic origin. This bone forms by the joining together of different primordia whose embryonic origins are dissimilar. The sphenoid bone has two distinct components. The cephalic mesoderm forms the basi-post-sphenoid and the orbitosphenoid, whereas the basi-pre-sphenoid and the alisphenoid have a neural crest cell origin. Several developmental diseases have links to the sphenoid bone, such as chordomas and trans-sphenoidal encephaloceles caused by the persistence of the craniopharyngeal canal. The first centers of ossification of the sphenoid appear in the eighth week of fetal life. These alae arise from the lateral walls of the skull. Later, the basisphenoid nuclei appear on the floor of the pituitary fossa while the lingulae appear between these two. [7]

retrieved_docs.content: The posterior wall of the maxillary sinus: within 1 cm anterior to the sphenopalatine foramen Maxillary line: 3.7 cm anterior to the sphenopalatine foramen Anterior head of the middle turbinate: 3.4 cm anterior to the sphenopalatine foramen Inferior turbinate horizontal lamina: 1.3 cm inferior to the sphenopalatine foramen Basal lamella: 1.2 cm anterior to the sphenopalatine foramen Choanal arch: 0.9 cm posterior to the sphenopalatine foramen Nasal floor: 1.8-2.7 cm inferior to the sphenopalatine foramen Ethmoid crest (i.e., crista ethmoidalis): a sharp bony projection that characterizes the anterior portion of the sphenopalatine foramen NOTE: Females have statistically shorter distances to the sphenopalatine foramen from the nasal floor, maxillary line, choanal arch, and anterior head of the middle turbinate. retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Ethmoid Bone -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The ethmoid bone is an unpaired cranial bone that is a significant component of the upper nasal cavity and the nasal septum. The ethmoid bone also constitutes the medial orbit wall.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Sphenopalatine Foramen -- Embryology
retrieved_docs.content: The sphenopalatine foramen forms from the fusion of the palatine bone and the sphenoid bone around the sphenopalatine artery; thus, a description of the embryology of the structures, as mentioned earlier, will provide a better understanding of the development of the sphenopalatine foramen.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Sphenoid Bone -- Structure and Function -- Openings
retrieved_docs.content: This cranial bone has several openings to allow the entry and exit of several nerves and blood vessels to move in and out of the cranial cavity. These include the optic canal, the superior orbital fissure, the foramen rotundum, the foramen lacerum, the foramen spinosum, and the foramen ovale. The foramen lacerum is between the sphenoid, the basilar part of the occipital, and the apex of the temporal bone and often contains connective tissue. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 1224
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to ethmoid bone, metastatic malignant neoplasm to frontal bone, and metastatic malignant neoplasm to temporal bone (diagnosis) are each classified as malignant neoplasms of the ethmoid bone, frontal bone, and temporal bone respectively. These site‑specific malignant bone tumors are encompassed within the broader diagnostic category “cancer of bone and connective tissue,” which is mapped to each of the three bone‑specific malignant neoplasms. Consequently, the three metastatic bone cancers are recognized as particular manifestations of bone and connective tissue cancer, highlighting their shared pathology while preserving their distinct anatomical locations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 2 -- Community 896
retrieved_docs.content: as “computed tomography of head: mastoid bone erosion of sigmoid sinus” uses the CT head as its finding method and localizes the abnormality to the sinus durae matris (dural venous sinus). This demonstrates how CT head imaging can precisely pinpoint bony and vascular lesions within the skull base. Clinically, the CT head examination is associated with سرطان الغدة الرئوية (lung gland carcinoma), indicating that findings on cranial CT may be relevant in the work‑up or staging of this pulmonary malignancy, possibly due to metastatic spread or paraneoplastic phenomena. Overall, routine chest radiography serves as a foundational reference point from which various advanced CT protocols are derived, all of which are integrated within the broader radiographic framework, linked to CNS imaging structures, and applied to both localized cranial pathology and systemic oncologic assessment.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 188

The neoplasm of the bone of the face is linked by an inverse‑isa relationship to the primary malignant neoplasm of the bone of the face, which is mapped to the broader category “cancer of bone and connective tissue.” This cancer category is broader than, and encompasses, the specific malignant neoplasms of the ethmoid bone, frontal bone, occipital bone, parietal bone, temporal bone, and vomer, and it is related to the general term for bone tumors (ورم العظام). The primary malignant neoplasm of the nasal bone is also mapped to the “cancer of bone and connective tissue” category, reinforcing the connection between individual bone‑site malignancies and the overarching classification of bone and connective‑tissue cancers. The Arabic term “سرطانة توتية” is linked through an inverse‑isa relationship to the concept of a primary malignant tumor, illustrating a semantic network that ties specific bone‑site cancers, metastatic processes, and general bone‑tumor classifications

The CT head modality itself has a broader relationship with rentgenografie, reinforcing its place within the overall X‑ray imaging spectrum. Within the central nervous system (CNS) imaging hierarchy, the CT head is a child procedure of the parent category إجراءات تصوير الجهاز العصبي المركزي (CNS imaging procedures). This parent category also includes other specialized studies, such as مخطط صدى الدماغ غير طبيعي (abnormal brain MRI plan), illustrating the range of neuro‑imaging techniques available for evaluating intracranial pathology. CT of the head is employed as a diagnostic method for identifying specific anatomical findings. For example, a CT study described as “computed tomography of head: mastoid bone erosion of sigmoid sinus” uses the CT head as its finding method and localizes the abnormality to the sinus durae matris (dural venous sinus). retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2459
retrieved_docs.content: Fracture of anterior fossa and fracture of middle fossa are both classified as إِصابة داخل الجمجمة (intracranial injury). Each represents a specific type of skull‑base fracture, with the anterior fossa fracture involving the front portion of the cranial base and the middle fossa fracture affecting the central region of the cranial base. Both conditions fall under the broader category of injuries inside the skull, highlighting their shared nature as intracranial traumatic lesions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 675
retrieved_docs.content: tomography of the head that reveals mastoid bone erosion of the sigmoid sinus. In that context, the finding site is the sinus durae matris (dural venous sinus), and the imaging method employed is the computerized axial tomography (CT) scan head. The central nervous system imaging category also encompasses the abnormal brain echo mapping procedure (مخطط صدى الدماغ غير طبيعي) as a child entity, illustrating the breadth of neuro‑imaging techniques linked to CT head imaging.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 2 -- Community 1151
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to ethmoid bone, metastatic malignant neoplasm to occipital bone, and metastatic malignant neoplasm to temporal bone (diagnosis) are each classified as malignant neoplasm of ethmoid bone, malignant neoplasm of occipital bone, and malignant neoplasm of temporal bone respectively. These site‑specific malignant bone tumors are encompassed within the broader diagnostic category of cancer of bone and connective tissue, to which each of the three bone‑specific neoplasms is mapped. Recognizing that metastatic lesions to the ethmoid, occipital, and temporal bones represent distinct manifestations of bone and connective tissue cancer aids clinicians in accurate coding, staging, and treatment planning for patients with skeletal metastases.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2299
retrieved_docs.content: Computed tomography of the head that demonstrates mastoid bone erosion of the sigmoid sinus identifies a pathological change located within the sinus durae matris, the dural venous sinus that houses the sigmoid sinus. Recognizing this imaging finding is important for evaluating mastoid disease and its potential impact on the dural venous drainage system.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 884
retrieved_docs.content: is identified as a possibly included method of exploration. Cranial exploration procedures include exploratory craniectomy or craniotomy of the infratentorial (posterior fossa) region, supratentorial craniectomy or craniotomy, and suboccipital craniectomy performed for exploration or decompression of cranial nerves. An orbit can be explored via a transcranial approach with lesion removal, which is likewise categorized as an exploration method. Spinal exploration is represented by laminectomy procedures that combine exploration and/or decompression of the spinal cord or cauda equina without additional facetectomy, foraminotomy, or discectomy. These laminectomies are specified for one or two lumbar vertebral segments (e.g., for spinal stenosis) and are noted as possibly included methods. Additional laminectomy variants cover more extensive cervical or lumbar segments (more than two vertebral levels) and are also listed as possibly included methods of exploration. Other organ‑specific

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 1187
retrieved_docs.content: surgery. Neurosurgical exploration includes both craniectomy and craniotomy performed for investigative purposes, with infratentorial (posterior fossa) and supratentorial variants identified as specific methods. Suboccipital craniectomy aimed at exploration or decompression of cranial nerves represents another dedicated technique, while transcranial orbital exploration with lesion removal extends the scope of exploratory surgery to the orbit. Spinal exploration is represented by laminectomy procedures that provide access to and decompression of the spinal cord or cauda equina without accompanying facetectomy, foraminotomy, or discectomy. These laminectomies are detailed for one or two vertebral segments in the cervical, lumbar (excluding spondylolisthesis), sacral, and thoracic regions, and for more than two segments in the cervical and thoracic spine, each described as a method that may be included in an overall exploration procedure. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 1065
retrieved_docs.content: Incision and excision of CNS is closely linked to the skull (lebka), as surgical access to the central nervous system typically requires opening or removing portions of the cranial bone. The skull (lebka) is also associated with a cranial nerve disorder (اضطراب بالعصب القحفي), reflecting the anatomical relationship between the bony structure and the nerves that traverse it. This cranial nerve disorder (اضطراب بالعصب القحفي) is categorized under the broader condition of ocular migraine (الشقيقة الشالة للعين), indicating that disturbances of cranial nerves can be manifestations of or related to ocular migraine. Recognizing how the procedure, skull anatomy, cranial nerve pathology, and ocular migraine interrelate aids clinicians in planning neurosurgical interventions, diagnosing nerve‑related symptoms, and providing comprehensive patient care.

Imaging of the head that shows mastoid bone erosion of the sigmoid sinus identifies the sinus durae matris as the site of the finding, further emphasizing the clinical relevance of dural structures in these pathologies.

A specific diagnostic application, “computed tomography of head: mastoid bone erosion of sinus durae matris,” uses head CT to locate pathology in the mastoid bone and the dural venous sinus. Head CT also co‑occurs with abdominal CT and is associated with “سرطان الغدة الرئوية,” underscoring its utility in comprehensive imaging assessments that may span lung, head, and abdominal regions. Overall, this structured taxonomy unifies bone density testing, a wide spectrum of X‑ray and CT examinations, genitourinary imaging, CNS imaging, and computer‑assisted surgical interventions within the comprehensive category of Other diagnostic radiology and related techniques. The hierarchical relationships clarify procedural classifications, support accurate coding and documentation, and aid clinicians in selecting appropriate imaging modalities across multilingual

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2680
retrieved_docs.content: Anterior epistaxis is identified as a disorder of the nasal septum (اضطراب في الحاجز الأنفي), representing bleeding that originates from the front portion of the nasal cavity and is commonly associated with structural or mucosal abnormalities of the septum.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2107
retrieved_docs.content: Skull and face fractures, described in Arabic as كسر في قبو القحف, refer to injuries involving breaks in the cranial vault and the facial bones, encompassing a range of traumatic damage to the head and facial structures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 2 -- Community 1207
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to ethmoid bone, metastatic malignant neoplasm to occipital bone, and metastatic malignant neoplasm to vomer are each classified as malignant neoplasm of ethmoid bone, malignant neoplasm of occipital bone, and malignant neoplasm of vomer respectively. These three site‑specific malignant neoplasms are encompassed within the broader category cancer of bone and connective tissue, which is also described by the Arabic term ورم العظام. Cancer of bone and connective tissue has a broader relationship with ورم العظام and is mapped to the malignant neoplasm of ethmoid bone, the malignant neoplasm of occipital bone, and the malignant neoplasm of vomer, indicating that metastatic malignant neoplasms of these cranial bones are considered subtypes of bone and connective tissue cancer. This integrated classification aids in understanding the common pathological basis of these tumors and supports coordinated diagnostic and therapeutic approaches.
